title: Chaetanthera acheno-hirsuta
intro: Chaetanthera acheno-hirsuta, commonly known as "South American chamomile", is a flowering plant species native to the Andes mountains in South America, characterized by its hairy achenes and daisy-like flowers. Traditionally, Chaetanthera acheno-hirsuta has been used in folk medicine for its purported health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and antiseptic properties, which are thought to soothe digestive issues and promote relaxation. The bioactive constituents of Chaetanthera acheno-hirsuta include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenes, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Chaetanthera acheno-hirsuta is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and is commonly used to treat digestive problems, anxiety, and insomnia.
taxonomy: Chaetanthera acheno-hirsuta is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Chaetanthera, which is a group of plants native to South America. The plant's classification places it within the subfamily Mutisioideae. Further classification groups it into the tribe Mutisieae, a group of flowering plants characterized by their daisy-like flowers. This classification is based on the plant's morphological and molecular characteristics.
